title: Vyaire VELA Ventilator - Power or Battery Failure
description: Guide for troubleshooting power or battery failures on Vyaire VELA ventilators with emphasis on external checks and patient safety.

steps.title: Verify AC Power Source
steps.instructions:
Ensure the ventilator is plugged into a known working outlet.
Check for tripped breakers or GFCI interruptions.
Why: A common cause of “power failure” is simply no AC supply.

steps.title: Inspect Power Cord and Connector
steps.instructions:
Look for fraying, damage, or bent pins.
Confirm the connector seats securely into the ventilator.
Why: Damaged cords or loose connections can prevent charging or powering on.

steps.title: Check Battery Status Indicator
steps.instructions:
Observe the battery LED or display icon.
If the battery is critically low, allow the device to charge for at least 30 minutes.
Why: Some failures occur because the battery is fully discharged and needs time to accept a charge.

steps.title: Swap or Test with Known Good Battery
steps.instructions:
If the device has a removable battery, insert a fully charged spare.
Why: Batteries degrade over time; a failing battery often mimics a device power failure.

steps.title: Attempt Power-On Without Accessories
steps.instructions:
Remove non-essential accessories (humidifier, external modules) and try to power on.
Why: Faulty accessories can sometimes prevent proper startup.

steps.title: Inspect Ventilator for External Damage
steps.instructions:
Check for signs of liquid ingress, physical damage, or unusual odors.
Why: External damage can affect the power circuitry and may require repair.

CCR.Complaint: Ventilator will not power on; battery indicator shows empty.
CCR.Cause: AC outlet confirmed functional; power cord intact; battery fully charged but ventilator still does not start.
CCR.Resolution: Device removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for bench evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Device removed from patient and labeled Out of Service

title: Vyaire VELA Ventilator - Alarm Not Triggering or Misconfigured
description: Guide for troubleshooting external alarm failures or misconfigurations on a Vyaire VELA ventilator, focusing on safety and proper setup.

steps.title: Verify Device Power and Battery
steps.instructions:
Ensure the ventilator is powered on and receiving adequate AC power or battery charge.
Reason: Alarm circuits may not function correctly if power is unstable or insufficient.

steps.title: Check Alarm Settings
steps.instructions:
Review alarm limits and parameters on the touchscreen or control panel.
Confirm that alarms are enabled and thresholds are appropriate for the patient.
Reason: Misconfigured settings are a common cause of alarms not triggering.

steps.title: Inspect Alarm Indicators
steps.instructions:
Confirm audible alarm volume is turned up and the visual indicators (LEDs or screen notifications) are functional.
Reason: Sometimes the alarm triggers internally but the user cannot perceive it.

steps.title: Verify Sensor and Circuit Connections
steps.instructions:
Check all patient circuit connections, pressure lines, and sensors for secure attachment.
Reason: Disconnected or loose sensors can prevent proper alarm detection.

steps.title: Perform a Self-Test / System Check
steps.instructions:
Run the built-in system diagnostic or alarm test mode if available.
Reason: Confirms internal alarm circuits and software logic are functional without risking patient safety.

steps.title: Swap with a Known Good Unit
steps.instructions: If possible, replace the ventilator with a tested device to ensure the issue is with the unit and not the environment or setup.

CCR.Complaint: Nurse reports ventilator fails to trigger high-pressure alarm during manual test.
CCR.Cause: Verified power, settings, and sensor connections; built-in self-test fails to sound alarm.
CCR.Resolution: Ventilator removed from service, labeled Out of Service, sent for bench evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Any unusual noises or indicator behavior

title: Vyaire VELA Ventilator - Circuit Leak or Tubing Disconnection Errors
description: Troubleshooting guide for Vyaire VELA ventilators experiencing circuit leak or tubing disconnection errors, focusing on external checks and patient safety.

steps.title: Verify Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Ensure the patient is stable and, if necessary, transfer to a backup ventilator before troubleshooting. Never attempt troubleshooting while the patient is dependent on the device.

steps.title: Check Circuit Connections
steps.instructions: Confirm all tubing connections (inspiratory, expiratory, and humidifier lines) are securely attached. Inspect for loose or partially inserted connectors at the patient interface and ventilator ports.

steps.title: Inspect Tubing for Damage
steps.instructions: Look for kinks, cracks, or holes in the breathing circuit tubing. Replace any tubing showing wear, tears, or discoloration.

steps.title: Examine Humidifier and Accessories
steps.instructions: Ensure the humidifier chamber is seated correctly and properly connected to the ventilator. Confirm that any bacterial/viral filters or inline sensors are correctly installed and not obstructed.

steps.title: Check for Obstructions or Moisture
steps.instructions: Remove any visible condensate or liquid buildup in the circuit, which can trigger false leak alarms. Verify that tubing is free of condensation traps or blockages.

steps.title: Confirm Ventilator Settings and Alarm Configuration
steps.instructions: Verify that the ventilator is set for the correct circuit type and patient parameters. Ensure leak detection alarms are enabled and appropriately set according to manufacturer guidelines.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Ventilator
steps.instructions: Safely disconnect the ventilator from AC power if not in active patient use and allow it to reboot. Observe for persistent alarms upon restart.

CCR.Complaint: User reports “High leak alarm” or “Circuit disconnected” message during ventilation.
CCR.Cause: Tubing found disconnected at expiratory port; inspiratory line kinked near humidifier chamber.
CCR.Resolution: Reconnected all tubing, replaced kinked section, verified secure connections, and confirmed leak alarm cleared.

helpfulDetails: Circuit tubing inspected for kinks or tears
Alarm behavior observed before and after intervention

title: Vyaire VELA Ventilator – Flow, Pressure, or Tidal Volume Delivery Discrepancies
description: Troubleshooting guide for inconsistent flow, pressure, or tidal volume delivery on a Vyaire VELA ventilator, focusing on external and easily verifiable checks.

steps.title: Verify Power and Battery
steps.instructions: Ensure the ventilator is properly powered and battery is charged. Check for battery low warnings or unexpected shutdowns. Why: Inadequate power can cause the ventilator to deliver inconsistent parameters.

steps.title: Inspect Circuit Connections
steps.instructions: Confirm all tubing, connectors, and patient interfaces are properly connected and secure. Check for kinks, loose connections, or condensation in the tubing. Why: Leaks or disconnections directly affect delivered flow, pressure, and tidal volume.

steps.title: Check Filters and Humidifiers
steps.instructions: Verify that bacterial/HEPA filters are clean and not obstructed. Ensure the humidifier (if used) is properly seated and operational. Why: Obstructions increase resistance, affecting volume and pressure delivery.

steps.title: Review Ventilator Settings
steps.instructions: Confirm clinician-prescribed settings match ventilator parameters (mode, tidal volume, PEEP, inspiratory time, FiO₂). Ensure no unintended changes or software errors have altered settings. Why: Misconfigured settings may appear as discrepancies in delivered parameters.

steps.title: Observe Patient Interface
steps.instructions: Inspect mask, endotracheal tube, or tracheostomy interface for leaks or poor fit. Check for signs of patient coughing, secretions, or obstruction. Why: Patient-related leaks or airway resistance can mimic ventilator delivery issues.

steps.title: Run Basic Self-Test
steps.instructions: Perform the ventilator’s built-in test routine following manufacturer instructions. Note any failures or warnings reported. Why: Self-tests help identify external versus internal faults.

CCR.Complaint: Ventilator tidal volumes are inconsistent and pressures fluctuate unexpectedly.
CCR.Cause: Observed secure tubing, clean filters, correct settings, but discrepancies persist. No external leaks or obstructions noted.
CCR.Resolution: Device removed from service, labeled Out of Service, sent for repair. Patient safely transferred to alternate ventilator.

helpfulDetails: Tubing and connectors checked for kinks or leaks
Any unusual sounds, heat, or odors observed

title: Vyaire VELA Ventilator - Sensor or Accessory Connection Errors
description: Guide for troubleshooting sensor or accessory connection errors on a VYAIRE VELA ventilator, focusing on external checks before internal failure assumptions.

steps.title: Verify device power
steps.instructions:
Ensure the ventilator is powered on and battery is sufficiently charged.
Reason: Sensors may fail to initialize if the device is underpowered.

steps.title: Inspect all accessory and sensor connections
steps.instructions:
Check that connectors for flow sensors, temperature probes, and any auxiliary modules are fully seated.
Look for bent pins, dirt, or debris.
Reason: Poor connections are the most common cause of sensor detection errors.

steps.title: Check accessory compatibility
steps.instructions:
Confirm that connected accessories are approved for use with VELA.
Reason: Non-compatible sensors may trigger connection errors or fail to communicate properly.

steps.title: Examine cables and tubing for damage
steps.instructions:
Look for cracks, fraying, or loose ends.
Reason: Damaged wiring or tubing can prevent accurate sensor readings.

steps.title: Swap sensors or accessories
steps.instructions:
If duplicates are available, temporarily swap the problematic accessory with a known good one.
Reason: Helps isolate whether the error is with the device or the sensor/accessory itself.

steps.title: Check software/configuration
steps.instructions:
Ensure ventilator settings correctly correspond to connected sensors.
Reset the device if needed to force re-initialization of modules.

CCR.Complaint: “Sensor not detected” alarm appears when connecting flow sensor.
CCR.Cause: Flow sensor connector not fully seated; pins slightly misaligned.
CCR.Resolution: Re-seated the flow sensor, verified proper detection, and cleared alarm.

title: Vyaire AirSep / Mobilaire Series Oxygen Concentrator - Low Oxygen Purity Alarms
description: Low oxygen purity alarms caused by airflow restriction, filter issues, environmental factors, or compressor performance problems.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the concentrator is actively supplying oxygen, immediately switch the patient to a backup oxygen source. Expected: Patient continues receiving appropriate oxygen therapy Why it matters: Low purity oxygen can result in inadequate oxygen delivery and patient harm

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm
steps.instructions: Power on the unit and observe the alarm behavior. Check: Low oxygen purity alarm present, audible and visual alarm indicators, and when the alarm occurs (startup vs continuous use). Expected: Alarm is confirmed and reproducible Why it matters: Confirms true purity issue vs transient startup condition

steps.title: Allow for Warm-Up Time (Common and Overlooked)
steps.instructions: Let the concentrator run for 5–10 minutes. Expected: Oxygen purity stabilizes and alarm clears Why it matters: Many concentrators trigger temporary purity alarms during startup before reaching operating levels

steps.title: Check Air Intake Filters (Most Common Cause)
steps.instructions: Inspect external and internal accessible filters. Remove and check for dust, lint, or blockage. Clean or replace per facility protocol. Expected: Clean filters allow proper airflow Why it matters: Restricted airflow reduces oxygen concentration efficiency

steps.title: Inspect Air Intake Area and Placement
steps.instructions: Ensure the unit has proper ventilation with clearance around intake vents and no obstruction. Expected: Unrestricted air intake Why it matters: Poor airflow reduces oxygen separation efficiency

steps.title: Check Oxygen Output Path
steps.instructions: Inspect tubing and delivery accessories. Ensure tubing is not kinked or occluded, remove humidifier bottle if present and retest, and check for leaks or loose connections. Expected: Smooth, unrestricted oxygen flow Why it matters: Back pressure or leaks can affect purity readings

steps.title: Verify Flow Rate Settings
steps.instructions: Ensure flow is within device-rated range and avoid maximum unless required. Expected: Proper flow within operational limits Why it matters: Excessive flow demand can reduce oxygen purity

steps.title: Assess Environmental Conditions
steps.instructions: Check for high humidity, extreme temperatures, or poor air quality. Expected: Normal indoor operating conditions Why it matters: Environmental factors impact concentrator efficiency

steps.title: Listen for Compressor Operation
steps.instructions: Listen for normal steady compressor sound and absence of abnormal noise. Expected: Compressor runs consistently Why it matters: Weak or failing compressor reduces oxygen production capability

steps.title: Reset and Retest the Unit
steps.instructions: Power cycle the concentrator and restart. Expected: Alarm clears if issue was temporary Why it matters: Clears minor faults and reinitializes system operation

CCR.Complaint: Unit alarming low oxygen purity during patient use.
CCR.Cause: Air intake filter heavily clogged, restricting airflow.
CCR.Resolution: Cleaned/replaced filter, verified oxygen output, and confirmed alarm cleared.

helpfulDetails: Unit placement and airflow clearance verified
Flow rate setting at time of issue

title: Vyaire AirSep / Mobilaire Series Oxygen Concentrator - Compressor Failure or Insufficient Flow
description: Low or no oxygen flow, weak output, or suspected compressor failure due to airflow restriction, power issues, or external component problems.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the unit is in use and flow is inadequate, immediately move the patient to a backup oxygen source. Expected: Patient continues receiving prescribed oxygen. Why it matters: Insufficient oxygen delivery can quickly compromise patient safety.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Power on the concentrator and observe: Is airflow weak or absent at the outlet? Are there alarms (low flow, pressure, or system fault)? Is the compressor attempting to run (audible hum or vibration)? Expected: Issue is clearly confirmed. Why it matters: Differentiates between no flow, low flow, or intermittent operation.

steps.title: Check Flowmeter Setting and Output (Most Common Cause)
steps.instructions: Verify the flowmeter is set correctly and not turned down or closed. Confirm prescribed flow rate. Check for obstruction at the outlet port. Expected: Proper flow setting with unobstructed output. Why it matters: Misadjusted flow or blockage can mimic compressor failure.

steps.title: Inspect External Tubing and Accessories
steps.instructions: Check all downstream components: Oxygen tubing not kinked or crushed. No occlusions or moisture buildup. Replace tubing if questionable. Expected: Clear, unobstructed airflow path. Why it matters: Restrictions reduce perceived flow even if compressor is functioning.

steps.title: Check Air Intake and Filters
steps.instructions: Inspect intake vents and filters: External intake filter clean and unobstructed. No dust buildup blocking airflow. Replace or clean filter per facility practice. Expected: Free airflow into the unit. Why it matters: Restricted intake reduces compressor efficiency and output.

steps.title: Listen for Compressor Operation
steps.instructions: With the unit powered on: Listen for compressor cycling or continuous run. Note abnormal sounds (loud knocking, no sound, or excessive vibration). Expected: Smooth, consistent compressor sound. Why it matters: No sound may indicate failure; abnormal noise may indicate wear or mechanical failure.

steps.title: Verify Power Source Stability
steps.instructions: Ensure the unit is receiving proper power: Plug into a known good outlet. Avoid extension cords or power strips. Confirm no intermittent power loss. Expected: Stable operation with consistent power. Why it matters: Low or unstable voltage can prevent compressor startup.

steps.title: Check for Overheating or Thermal Shutdown
steps.instructions: Inspect the unit: Is the unit excessively warm? Are vents blocked? Has the unit recently shut down and restarted? Allow unit to cool if overheated. Expected: Normal operating temperature. Why it matters: Overheating can cause compressor shutdown or reduced output.

steps.title: Inspect for System Alarms or Indicators
steps.instructions: Check for: Low pressure or system fault alarms. Service indicator lights. Hour meter indicating maintenance interval reached. Expected: No active fault indicators. Why it matters: Alerts may indicate internal compressor or system degradation.

CCR.Complaint: Oxygen concentrator reported low flow and inadequate oxygen delivery.
CCR.Cause: Compressor running weak due to restricted intake filter causing reduced airflow.
CCR.Resolution: Cleaned/replaced intake filter, verified proper airflow and normal unit operation.

helpfulDetails: Final device status (returned to service or removed)

title: Vyaire AirSep / Mobilaire Series Oxygen Concentrator - Power or Battery Backup Failure
description: Troubleshooting power loss, shutdowns, or battery backup failure in Vyaire AirSep and Mobilaire oxygen concentrators.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the concentrator loses power, immediately transition the patient to an alternate oxygen source such as a backup concentrator or oxygen tank.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Attempt to power on the unit and observe for no power, intermittent shutdown, AC-only operation, or battery alarms.

steps.title: Check AC Power Source (Most Common Cause)
steps.instructions: Plug the unit into a known working outlet, test with another device, and avoid using power strips or extension cords.

steps.title: Inspect Power Cord and Connections
steps.instructions: Ensure the cord is fully seated, inspect for damage, and try a known-good power cord if detachable.

steps.title: Check Power Indicator Lights and Display
steps.instructions: Verify power LED and display are active and check for warning or error indicators.

steps.title: Test Battery Backup Function (If Equipped)
steps.instructions: Disconnect AC power and observe if the unit continues running or shuts off and check for battery alarms.

steps.title: Allow Battery to Charge Fully
steps.instructions: Reconnect AC power, allow full charging time, and verify charging indicator if present.

steps.title: Inspect External Airflow and Ventilation
steps.instructions: Check intake vents and filters, ensure airflow is not blocked, and clean or replace filters if needed.

steps.title: Check for Signs of Internal Power Issues
steps.instructions: Observe for burning smell, excessive heat, or unusual noises indicating internal failure.

CCR.Complaint: Oxygen concentrator not powering on and failed to operate during transport.
CCR.Cause: Failed internal battery pack not holding charge; AC power verified functional.
CCR.Resolution: Removed unit from service and sent for battery replacement and power system evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Signs of heat, smell, or noise

title: Vyaire AirSep / Mobilaire Series Oxygen Concentrator - Hour Meter or Service Indicator Errors
description: Addresses incorrect hour meter readings or service indicator alerts caused by power issues, resets, or normal maintenance intervals.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the concentrator is actively supplying oxygen, transfer the patient to a backup oxygen source before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Power on the unit and observe the display or indicator panel to determine if the hour meter is frozen, inaccurate, or reset, and if a service indicator is illuminated.

steps.title: Check for Normal Maintenance Interval (Most Common Cause)
steps.instructions: Review the unit’s service interval expectations and determine if the unit has reached a typical service threshold using maintenance logs or PM stickers.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Unit
steps.instructions: Turn the concentrator off, unplug from AC power, wait 30–60 seconds, then restart.

steps.title: Verify Power Stability
steps.instructions: Plug into a known good outlet, avoid extension cords, and observe for flickering display or intermittent resets.

steps.title: Inspect Control Panel and Display
steps.instructions: Check for dim, flickering, or partially missing display segments and unresponsive buttons.

steps.title: Attempt Service Indicator Reset (If Applicable)
steps.instructions: Follow facility-approved reset procedure if available without accessing internal components.

steps.title: Check for Additional Performance Issues
steps.instructions: Observe concentrator operation for normal oxygen flow, absence of abnormal noise, heat, or alarms.

CCR.Complaint: Unit displaying service indicator and incorrect hour meter reading.
CCR.Cause: Service interval reached; indicator not reset after maintenance.
CCR.Resolution: Verified operation, reset service indicator per procedure, returned to service.

title: Vyaire AirSep / Mobilaire Series Oxygen Concentrator - Filter Blockage or Airflow Restriction
description: Reduced airflow, low output, overheating, or alarms caused by clogged filters or restricted air intake/exhaust.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the concentrator is in use, move the patient to a backup oxygen source before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Confirm reduced airflow output, low oxygen purity or flow alarms, and increased noise or heat.

steps.title: Check External Air Intake (Most Common Cause)
steps.instructions: Ensure vents are not blocked and confirm proper clearance around the unit.

steps.title: Inspect and Clean External Filters
steps.instructions: Check for debris and clean filters per protocol, then reinstall.

steps.title: Check Internal/Secondary Filters (If Accessible Without Disassembly)
steps.instructions: Inspect and replace secondary filters if dirty.

steps.title: Inspect Exhaust Venting
steps.instructions: Ensure exhaust areas are unobstructed and warm air is discharged.

steps.title: Check for Environmental Factors
steps.instructions: Evaluate for dust, lint, or poor placement restricting airflow.

steps.title: Re-test Unit Performance
steps.instructions: Power on and confirm normal airflow and alarm clearance.

CCR.Complaint: Oxygen concentrator alarming for low flow and reduced output.
CCR.Cause: Intake filter heavily clogged with dust restricting airflow.
CCR.Resolution: Cleaned filter, verified proper airflow and normal operation.

helpfulDetails: No abnormal noises beyond normal compressor operation
